problems.question: Compare: 3/8 ○ 5/8

feedback.incorrect: Same denominator (8). Compare numerators: 3 < 5, so 3/8 < 5/8.

hints: Same denominator — compare numerators.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which fraction is greater: 4/6 or 2/6?

feedback.incorrect: To find equivalent: 2/3 × 2/2 = 4/6.

hints: Same denominator — larger numerator = greater fraction.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Compare: 1/3 ○ 1/6

feedback.correct: Right! Same numerator: smaller denominator → larger pieces, so 1/3 > 1/5.
feedback.incorrect: Same numerator. Smaller denominator means bigger pieces: 1/3 > 1/5.

hints: Same numerator — smaller denominator = greater fraction.
problems.feedback_correct: Right! Same numerator: smaller denominator → larger pieces, so 1/3 > 1/5.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which fraction is smaller: 1/4 or 1/2?

feedback.incorrect: Same numerator (1). Smaller denominator (4) → bigger piece: 1/4 > 1/8.

hints: Same numerator — larger denominator = smaller fraction.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Tom ran 5/8 of a mile. Sara ran 3/8 of a mile. Who ran farther?
choices: C) They ran the same distance

feedback.correct: Right — same denominator, larger numerator means more.
feedback.incorrect: Same denominator (5). 3/5 > 2/5, so Tom ate more.

hints: Same denominator — compare numerators: 5 > 3.
problems.feedback_correct: Right — same denominator, larger numerator means more.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Compare: 1/2 ○ 1/8

feedback.correct: Correct! Larger pieces means greater value.
feedback.incorrect: Same numerator (1). 1/2 > 1/4 because halves are bigger than fourths.

hints: Same numerator — smaller denominator = bigger piece.
problems.feedback_correct: Correct! Larger pieces means greater value.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Compare: 2/3 ○ 3/4

feedback.correct: Right! 1/3 < 1/2 — compare to the half benchmark.
feedback.incorrect: Compare to 1/2: 1/3 is less than 1/2. So 1/3 < 1/2.

hints: Use a number line or benchmark 1/2 to compare.
problems.feedback_correct: Right! 1/3 < 1/2 — compare to the half benchmark.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which fraction is greater: 3/4 or 2/3?

feedback.incorrect: Use 1/2 as a benchmark: 3/4 > 1/2 > 1/3, so 3/4 > 1/3.

hints: 3/4 is close to 1 whole. 2/3 is also close but not as close.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Order from least to greatest: 3/4, 1/4, 2/4

feedback.correct: Right — same denominator, just compare numerators.
feedback.incorrect: Same denominator (4). Order numerators: 1 < 2 < 3.

hints: Same denominator — order by numerator.
problems.feedback_correct: Right — same denominator, just compare numerators.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Order from greatest to least: 1/2, 1/6, 1/3

feedback.correct: Yes — same numerator, larger denominator → smaller fraction.
feedback.incorrect: Same numerator. Larger denominator means smaller piece: 1/2 > 1/3 > 1/6.

hints: Same numerator — smaller denominator = greater fraction.
problems.feedback_correct: Yes — same numerator, larger denominator → smaller fraction.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?

feedback.incorrect: Two equal models: 1/2 = 2/4.

hints: Equivalent fractions name the same amount. 1/2 = ?/4

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which pair shows equivalent fractions?

feedback.incorrect: 1/3 = 2/6 (×2 top and bottom).

hints: Equivalent fractions cover the same area of a whole.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 2/4?

feedback.incorrect: Simplify 2/4: divide top and bottom by 2 → 1/2.

hints: 2/4 and 1/2 name the same point on a number line.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Compare: 2/4 ○ 1/2

feedback.incorrect: 3/6 simplifies to 1/2; both equal one half.

hints: 2/4 and 1/2 are equivalent fractions.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Maya ate 3/6 of a granola bar. Leo ate 4/6 of the same size bar. Who ate more?
choices: C) They ate the same amount

feedback.incorrect: Same denominator (5). 4 > 3, so Leo had more.

hints: Same denominator — compare numerators: 4 > 3.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Aiden drank 1/3 of a bottle of water. Emma drank 1/4 of the same size bottle. Who drank more?
choices: C) They drank the same amount

feedback.correct: Yes — Aiden ate 1/4, biggest pieces with same numerator 1.
feedback.incorrect: Same numerator (1). Smallest denominator wins: 1/4 > 1/6 > 1/8.

hints: Same numerator — 1/3 has a smaller denominator so each piece is bigger.
problems.feedback_correct: Yes — Aiden ate 1/4, biggest pieces with same numerator 1.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Order from least to greatest: 1/2, 1/8, 1/4, 1/3

feedback.correct: Right — smallest piece has biggest denominator.
feedback.incorrect: Same numerator (1). Order from smallest to biggest piece: 1/8 < 1/4 < 1/3 < 1/2.

hints: Same numerator — order by denominator from greatest to least denominator = least to greatest fraction.
problems.feedback_correct: Right — smallest piece has biggest denominator.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Which TWO fractions are equivalent to 1/2?

feedback.incorrect: Check: 2/4 = 1/2 and 3/6 = 1/2. Both pairs equivalent.

hints: 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6. Each names the same amount.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Jake says 3/4 > 5/6 because 4 < 6. Is Jake correct? Why?
choices: A) Yes — smaller denominator always means greater fraction
B) No — you cannot compare fractions with different numerators AND denominators this way
C) Yes — 3/4 is always greater than any fraction with 6 in the denominator

feedback.correct: Correct — Jake's rule only works when numerators match. 5/6 > 3/4.
feedback.incorrect: Compare 3/4 = 9/12 vs 5/6 = 10/12. So 5/6 > 3/4. Jake's rule is wrong.

hints: Jake's rule only works when numerators are the same. Use a number line to compare 3/4 and 5/6.
problems.feedback_correct: Correct — Jake's rule only works when numerators match. 5/6 > 3/4.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

problems.question: Three friends ran different distances: Ana ran 3/4 mile, Ben ran 2/3 mile, and Cara ran 5/6 mile. Who ran the farthest?

feedback.incorrect: Compare to 1 whole: 5/6 closest, then 3/4, then 2/3. Cara ran the farthest.

hints: Compare each to the benchmark 1 whole: 5/6 is closest to 1.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.9 Lesson — U9_compare_fractions/unit_test
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: Nate says 2/4 = 2/8 because they both have a numerator of 2. Is he correct? Use a model to explain.
pretest.correct_answer: Nate is NOT correct. If you draw two same-size strips, 2/4 covers half the strip but 2/8 only covers a quarter. The shaded areas are different sizes. Same numerator does NOT mean equivalent — the denominators make the pieces different sizes.
hints: Same denominator → bigger numerator wins. Same numerator → smaller denominator means bigger pieces.

feedback.correct: Correct! The answer is Nate is NOT correct. If you draw two same-size strips, 2/4 covers half the strip but 2/8 only covers a quarter. The shaded areas are different sizes. Same numerator does NOT mean equivalent — the denominators make the pieces different sizes.
feedback.incorrect: The answer is Nate is NOT correct. If you draw two same-size strips, 2/4 covers half the strip but 2/8 only covers a quarter. The shaded areas are different sizes. Same numerator does NOT mean equivalent — the denominators make the pieces different sizes. Read the problem twice. Identify what's given and what's asked, then choose an operation.

pretest.feedback_correct: Correct! The answer is Nate is NOT correct. If you draw two same-size strips, 2/4 covers half the strip but 2/8 only covers a quarter. The shaded areas are different sizes. Same numerator does NOT mean equivalent — the denominators make the pieces different sizes.
